ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for packaged less-sugar winter melon drink; closest match is winter melon beverage (FOODON_03301638). Packaged and reduced-sugar attributes are not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Non-alcoholic beverages — flavoured water and plant-based drinks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for winter melon drink. A0FKP (plant-based non-alcoholic beverage) is the closest defensible anchor. Less-sugar and packaged attributes are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for packaged winter melon drink. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular beverage product codes at this level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve packaged winter melon drink is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to one standard can or small bottle as commonly sold in Singapore convenience stores and supermarkets.

nutrient_source.title: Winter Melon Drink (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for winter melon drink (less sugar, packaged), cr_id D05010043. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for packaged winter melon drink (less sugar). GI estimated at 52 using sucrose-sweetened dilute beverage analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database (dilute sugar-sweetened beverage, GI ~52, low category). The drink contains 7g carbohydrate per 100g (predominantly 6.2g sugar). GL per 250g serving = (52 × 17.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.1. This is classified as low GI but moderate GL per serving, consistent with SSB guidance.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.2g sugar/100g) exceeding the 5g/100g beverage sugar threshold; SSBs independently raise blood pressure risk via fructose-driven sodium retention and endothelial dysfunction regardless of low sodium content (13mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Persons with hypertension should avoid this beverage as it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 6.2g sugar per 100g; the AHA 2018 guidelines identify SSBs as an independent cardiovascular and blood pressure risk factor, and the 'less sugar' label does not confer suitability — replace with plain water, unsweetened herbal tea, or plain winter melon tea brewed without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid sugar (6.2g sugar/100g) with zero dietary fibre causes rapid postprandial glucose absorption; all sugar-sweetened beverages are contraindicated for Type 2 Diabetes management per ADA Standards of Medical Care regardless of reduced-sugar labelling.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should not consume this beverage; it contains 6.2g of rapidly absorbed liquid sugar per 100g with no fibre to attenuate glucose response, and the ADA guidelines classify all SSBs as unsuitable for diabetes management — choose plain water or unsweetened beverages only.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular SSB consumption is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular event risk; this beverage provides 6.2g sugar per 100g with no protective nutrients (zero fibre, zero beneficial fats, zero antioxidants).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Persons with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage; habitual SSB intake is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines, and the absence of any cardioprotective nutrients means this drink provides no cardiovascular benefit — replace with plain water or unsweetened beverages.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with zero fibre and zero anti-inflammatory nutrients promotes systemic inflammation relevant to RA disease activity; no positive anti-inflammatory factors are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Persons with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage as added sugar in SSBs promotes pro-inflammatory mediators and no anti-inflammatory factors (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants, whole-food fibre) are present — replace with plain water or unsweetened green tea.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Winter Melon Drink (Less Sugar, Packaged) is a ready-to-drink (RTD) commercially produced beverage popular in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is made from water, winter melon (Benincasa hispida) extract, and a reduced quantity of added sugar, then packaged in cans or bottles for retail sale. The drink has a mild, lightly sweet flavour with subtle herbal and gourd-like notes characteristic of winter melon. Its texture is thin and watery, similar to diluted fruit juice. Nutritionally, it provides 28 kcal per 100g, with 7g of carbohydrate (predominantly 6.2g sugar) and negligible protein and fat. Sodium is low at 13mg per 100g. Despite the 'less sugar' label, this beverage remains a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) by clinical definition, as it contains added sugar above the 5g/100g threshold used in clinical dietary guidelines. It is therefore unsuitable for persons managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The drink is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a refreshing cold drink in hot weather and is widely available in Singapore hawker centres, convenience stores, and supermarkets. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low GI) based on analogous dilute sugar-sweetened beverage data, but the glycemic load per 250ml serving is approximately 9.1, which is clinically meaningful for blood glucose management. Persons seeking a similar flavour without the sugar load should opt for unsweetened winter melon tea brewed at home without added sugar.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink winter melon drink?
Is packaged winter melon drink suitable for hypertension?
How many calories in winter melon drink?
Is less sugar winter melon drink sugar-free?
Is winter melon tea the same as winter melon drink?
How much sugar is in packaged winter melon drink?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D05010043) with a reported confidence of 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and consistent with the food type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as no direct measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: protein_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this is a carbohydrate-based sugar-sweetened beverage with no protein-contributing ingredients by formulation. fat_g and saturated_fat_g are confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — this beverage contains no fat-contributing ingredients by formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database — the beverage is a filtered liquid extract with no intact plant fibre. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal-derived ingredients. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this packaged beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for D05010043 as of 2026. GI value of 52 is an analogous estimate based on dilute sugar-sweetened beverage data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources. GL per 250g serving calculated as (52 × 17.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 9.1. The 'less sugar' label does not confer clinical suitability for diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease management — this beverage remains an SSB by all major clinical guideline definitions.
